tier1_posts.text: @thePartyPartyUS I honestly cannot figure out you are imagining. Are you imagining that somehow the information will be so perfect and complete that people will never draw the wrong conclusions in harmful ways? Do you understand why our legal system treats as fundamental the right to confront

@thePartyPartyUS If I'm going to murder my neighbor, I know I need to keep that secret. But I probably wouldn't realize that googling information about suicide for a night course or showing my grandson some shiny motorcycles was something I needed to hide.

@thePartyPartyUS Are you arguing that there will be this huge benefit to offset the dystopian harm because people will no longer be able to do evil in secret? Because I think people will still be able to hide the big, outright evil stuff because they'll know they have to.

@thePartyPartyUS Say one of my coworkers decides not to go to a social event with me because they found out that I searched for a lot of information about suicide, not realizing that I did so for a paper I was writing for a course I was taking. Are you imagining I'd somehow be entitled to find

@thePartyPartyUS I suspect you are imagining a world different from the one we currently live in and asking what would be so bad about that world. I don't see how anyone can answer that without understanding how you imagine that world. But it sounds like its legal system is nothing like ours.

@thePartyPartyUS I don't think you understand what I'm saying at all becase your response is insane. On what basis would I ever seek, or be entitled to, recourse? Are you imagining some world where it is legally wrongful to draw reasonable inferences that happen to be false?

@thePartyPartyUS It really comes down to what kind of world you want to live in and how much dystopia you think is acceptable.

Much of the internet's revenue model is based on advertisements and the value of an ad showing depends heavily on how much the platform knows about you. And because we

@MitraHispana @nypost Your argument is that the substance (what it's actually doing) doesn't matter and only the form (what it seems to be doing) matters. But I don't see how you can square that with how we look at everything other than AI.

Also, I'd argue that practicing law is actually interpreted

@MitraHispana @nypost How do you know that it makes recommendations and applies reasoning rather than just appearing to do so just as a search engine might to someone who didn't know what the internet was or how a search engine worked?

@MitraHispana @nypost The substance is that AI does not have legal knowledge, does not engage in legal reasoning, and does not make recommendations any more than a search engine does. I think the only way you can say it's practicing law is you look only at the form.

@MitraHispana @nypost It's not though. It's not engaging in legal reasoning. It's not applying legal knowledge. It's not actually recommending anything to anyone any more than a search engine that produces a hit on a page that says "I recommend that you do X" is recommending that the person searching

@love_dem_birds Very unlikely events happen all the time. If you include enough details, every event becomes unbelievably unlikely. I flip a coin and it comes up heads, 50%. But that it took the exact path it did and landed precisely as it did? Astronomically unlikely.

@COOLBREEZE_____ @LundukeJournal It means that whether or not copyright is infringed would be the same analysis whether the image was created by an AI or by a human. The test would be whether the final result contains substantial protected expression found in any prior body of work the process had access to.

@MitraHispana @nypost Practicing law is about the process. If we agree a search engine cannot practice law, then I think you must admit that you cannot tell if law is being practiced just by looking at the input and output. You have to look what is going on internally. Is there reasoning? Judgment?

@MitraHispana @nypost To someone not familiar with how search engines work, a search engine well might appear to exercise judgment or analyze particular facts. But AI does not exercise judgment or analyze particular facts because those are subjective intentional functions. It just appears to.

@MitraHispana @nypost To distill it down, you say, "I have <X> problem with a will" and a person gives you back "see book <Y>". That is definitely not practicing law if the person just knows that book <Y> has to do with wills and ignores the specifics.

But that is practicing law if the person thinks

@MitraHispana @nypost I'm not trying to have anything both ways. As I've consistently said, AI is an inanimate tool that humans put information into and humans use to get information out, like a gun, a book, or a search engine. The law absolutely lets you replace humans if the thing you're replacing

@MitraHispana @nypost It does not make recommendations any more than a book does. It provides text that looks similar to humans providing recommendations, but so does a book. No actual recommending is going on because to "recommend" something is to have a particular intent and mental state.

@MitraHispana @nypost It isn't making recommendations because to "recommend" something is to have a particular subjective intent that an AI cannot have. Again, it's an inanimate tool with prior human design then under its user's control.

probability.last_reasoning: Bear case probability nearly doubled from 10% to 18% due to the Hormuz disruption event and CRITICAL compound stress. A sustained oil shock above $100 threatens global recession, crypto winter, and XRP ETF outflows. However, the thesis paradox is now fully active: this exact crisis scenario is what the institutional settlement infrastructure thesis predicts would accelerate demand for efficient cross-border settlement. Mid-case actually increases slightly from 38% to 30% (net down) because if the crisis resolves quickly (Trump says oil will 'drop rapidly' after Iran), the infrastructure thesis emerges validated. Base case narrows. Bull case decreases because the macro damage from even a brief oil shock at $118 creates lasting headwinds.

bear_case.score_reasoning: Elevated from prior baseline. The oil shock at $118 threatens global recession which would suppress cross-border payment volumes regardless of infrastructure quality. If this escalation leads to a sustained >$100 oil environment, institutional risk appetite for crypto adoption collapses. The 17-day zero-adoption PermissionedDEX is a concrete infrastructure concern.

competing_infrastructure.name: SWIFT GPI
competing_infrastructure.status_text: In a crisis, institutions default to known infrastructure. SWIFT's 11,500-bank network is the incumbent during volatility — institutional conservatism benefits SWIFT during exactly this type of geopolitical stress.

competing_infrastructure.name: Visa B2B Connect
competing_infrastructure.status_text: No material updates. Visa's cross-border B2B product remains live but not expanding aggressively into XRPL's target corridors.

competing_infrastructure.name: JPMorgan Kinexys
competing_infrastructure.status_text: JPMorgan's institutional network has real volume and trust. In a crisis environment where counterparty risk matters, JPMorgan's balance sheet backing is a competitive advantage XRPL cannot match.

competing_infrastructure.name: BIS Project Nexus
competing_infrastructure.status_text: BIS multi-CBDC bridge projects continue development. A Hormuz crisis could accelerate central bank desire for sovereign settlement rails that explicitly exclude private tokens like XRP.

competing_infrastructure.name: Ethereum Institutional
competing_infrastructure.status_text: ETH down sharply in the risk-off. BlackRock's BUIDL fund and Ethereum institutional infrastructure exist but face same macro headwinds as XRPL.

bear_case.odl_stagnation: ODL volume data remains completely opaque — the longest-running data gap in the framework. Without this data, the core utility thesis is unfalsifiable and unverifiable, which is itself a bear signal.
bear_case.token_velocity_concern: No token velocity data available. If XRP is primarily used as a speculative asset rather than held for settlement utility, the price thesis disconnects from the adoption thesis. The -1% hold in a crisis could indicate either institutional conviction or simple illiquidity.

macro_headwinds.name: Global Recession Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Oil at $118 is stagflationary. Combined with tariffs and weakening consumer sentiment, recession probability has increased materially. A recession suppresses cross-border payment volumes — the core demand driver for XRP utility.

macro_headwinds.name: Crypto Winter Signals
macro_headwinds.status_text: Fear & Greed at 8, BTC below $66K, $120M liquidated in 60 minutes, silver crashing 5%. This has the signature of a broad risk-asset capitulation event. If sustained, crypto winter conditions would freeze institutional adoption timelines.

macro_headwinds.name: Regulatory Reversal Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Trump cyber strategy supporting crypto is positive. However, in a crisis, regulatory priorities shift to stability — potential for emergency restrictions on crypto markets if volatility becomes systemic.

macro_headwinds.name: Rate Hike Extension
macro_headwinds.status_text: Oil at $118 is inflationary. If inflation re-accelerates, the Fed cannot cut and may need to hold or even hike. Higher-for-longer rates suppress risk asset valuations and increase the opportunity cost of holding crypto.

bear_case.bear_narrative: Compound stress has jumped to CRITICAL in a single cycle: Brent crude at $118 demolished the $95 threshold by 24%, triggering cross-asset liquidation cascades ($120M crypto in 60 minutes, BTC to $66K, F&G at 8). The operator's action window is days, not weeks — Japanese markets have not yet priced in the oil shock, and USD/JPY at 157.92 pre-shock is already at elevated. Call is INCREASE_MONITORING with five defined trigger points for escalation to REDUCE_EXPOSURE. Beyond the acute crisis, a structural finding demands attention: Ripple's own stablecoin circulates 78% on Ethereum vs 22% on XRPL, converging with thin XRPL DEX volume ($1.63M/24h) to suggest organic market preference currently favors Ethereum over XRPL as execution infrastructure — this is internal evidence challenging the settlement layer thesis that no amount of favorable regulatory posture can offset. The thesis paradox is fully active: the oil-driven macro crisis that would most validate the need for resilient settlement alternatives is the same crisis that prevents their adoption.
